answer.rationale: Isaiah 55:11 binds davar (H1697) to two verbs of non-failure: shuv reqam negated ('shall not return empty') and hitsliah (H6743, Hifil of tsalach, 'cause to succeed/prosper') plus asah (H6213, 'accomplish'), giving the word an effectual force that cannot fall short of its sender's intent. Matt 5:18 / Luke 16:17 set heaven and earth against iota (G2503) and keraia (G2762) and use parerchomai (G3928) plus genetai (G1096) and pesein (G4098, 'fall/fail') to make even orthographic minutiae more durable than the cosmos until everything is accomplished. John 10:35 closes the loop with lyo (G3089) aorist passive infinitive plus modal impossibility: ou dynatai lythenai he graphe. The pan-canonical lemma network (asah/tsalach/davar + parerchomai/pipto/lyo applied to graphe and nomos) demonstrates infallibility as Scripture's own self-witness, not as a confessional construct.
answer.notes: Infallibility (cannot fail / cannot mislead in faith and practice) is the narrower and more lexically secure twin of inerrancy. The question stem distinguishes two strands: (a) effectual non-failure (Isa 55:11), and (b) non-misleading character in matters of faith and practice (Matt 5:18, Luke 16:17, John 10:35, 2 Tim 3:16-17, 2 Pet 1:20-21). Both stand on direct pan-canonical lexical evidence; the doctrine is not a single-passage inference. Public-record carriers of denial: Mormonism (Joseph Smith's 8th Article of Faith treats the Bible as the word of God 'as far as it is translated correctly' and authorizes Book of Mormon and ongoing revelation as correctives, implying the canonical text can mislead); Jehovah's Witnesses (Watchtower hierarchy claims the right to redefine prior canonical readings, treating the apostolic text alone as insufficient apart from the Governing Body); Iglesia ni Cristo (Felix Manalo doctrine that the Bible without his apostolic interpretation misleads); Christian Science (Mary Baker Eddy treats Scripture's historical narratives as parabolic and requiring Science and Health as decoder, implying their plain sense can mislead); liberal-Protestant strands that grant Scripture spiritual value but deny it can bind conscience on faith and practice. Fruit-vs-precision nuance: many sincere Christians use the word 'infallible' interchangeably with 'inerrant' or vice versa; the lexical case here is for the narrower proposition that Scripture cannot fail in its purpose and cannot mislead in faith and practice, which is the floor every historic lineage affirms.

stem_audit.notes: Statement uses 'cannot fail' and 'incapable of misleading' which are doctrinal claims, not heretic-naming. The Reformed-confessional phrase 'faith and practice' is also the Anglican Article 6, Methodist Article 5, and AG Statement 1 phrase; functionally trans-confessional, not lineage-loaded. No neutralization needed.

lay_summary.reasoning: Verdict: Scripture cannot fail to do what God sent it to do, and it cannot mislead anyone who is trying to learn from it how to be right with God and how to live. The strongest reason from Scripture is Isaiah 55:11, where God says his word does not return to him empty but accomplishes what he wants and succeeds in what he sent it for. Jesus himself reinforces this twice. In Matthew 5:18 he says heaven and earth will pass away before the smallest letter or smallest stroke of a pen of the Law passes away until everything is fulfilled. In Luke 16:17 he says it is easier for heaven and earth to disappear than for one tiny stroke of the Law to fall, and the Greek word translated 'fall' is the same word used for failing. In John 10:35 Jesus rests an entire argument on a single phrase in the Psalms and says 'Scripture cannot be broken.' Paul adds that all Scripture is breathed out by God and is profitable for teaching, correcting, and training in righteousness so that the believer is fully equipped (2 Timothy 3:16 to 17), and Peter says no prophecy of Scripture came from the will of man, but men were carried along by the Holy Spirit (2 Peter 1:21). The strongest counterargument inside the Bible itself is that Scripture sometimes uses everyday observational speech (Joshua 10:13, the sun stood still) or contains numerical differences between Kings and Chronicles, or that Mark 13:32 says even Jesus did not know the day or the hour. Critics ask, if Scripture can have these features, how can it be incapable of misleading. The verdict handles this honestly by limiting the claim to what Scripture itself is teaching about God, salvation, and how to live. Observational language is not a science textbook. Numerical variations are scribal transmission. The Mark 13:32 limitation is voluntary kenosis during the incarnation, not a Scripture-error. The claim that Scripture cannot fail in its purpose and cannot mislead in faith and practice is what every page of the Bible assumes about itself when it cites earlier Scripture as binding.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: Every major historic Christian lineage affirms Scripture's infallibility in matters of faith and practice. The patristic fathers (Irenaeus, Athanasius, Augustine in Letter 82 to Jerome) believed Scripture's authors were completely free from error in the canonical books. The Catholic Church in Vatican II's Dei Verbum 11 confesses that Scripture teaches 'solidly, faithfully and without error that truth which God wanted put into sacred writings for the sake of salvation.' Lutherans in the Formula of Concord call the prophetic and apostolic writings the only rule and norm by which all doctrine is judged. Anglicans in Article 6 of the 39 Articles teach that Scripture contains all things necessary to salvation. The Reformed confessions (Westminster 1.5, 1689 Baptist 1.5, Belgic, Heidelberg) confess Scripture's full divine authority and self-evidencing infallibility. Methodists in their Article 5 follow the Anglican formulation. Anabaptists at Schleitheim and after treat Scripture as the binding standard. Pentecostals in the Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths Article 1 confess Scripture as 'the inspired, infallible, and authoritative rule of faith and conduct.' Eastern Orthodoxy confesses Scripture as the divinely inspired word of God within Holy Tradition. Carriers of denial place themselves outside historic Christianity. Mormonism in its 8th Article of Faith treats the Bible as authoritative only 'as far as it is translated correctly' and adds the Book of Mormon plus living-prophet revelation as correctives, which presumes the canonical text can mislead. Jehovah's Witnesses treat the Governing Body as the necessary interpreter without which the Bible misleads, and their New World Translation distorts Christological passages. Iglesia ni Cristo, Christian Science under Mary Baker Eddy, and other modern restorationist movements likewise treat the plain canonical text as insufficient or misleading apart from their founder's correction. Some liberal-academic Protestant positions reduce Scripture to ancient religious literature without binding force on faith and practice, which is a denial of the historic Christian confession on this point.

scripture.key_term: דָּבָר (davar) H1697; שׁוּב (shuv) H7725; עָשָׂה (asah) H6213; צָלַח (tsalach) H6743 in Hifil
scripture.force: Davar = utterance, word, message. Shuv reqam negated, 'shall not return empty', and the Hifil of tsalach (hitsliah, 'cause to succeed/prosper') paired with asah ('accomplish') give God's word a categorical effectual force: it cannot fall short of the sender's purpose. The same verb-pair (asah + tsalach) is used elsewhere of guaranteed success (Josh 1:8, Ps 1:3)

scripture.key_term: ἰῶτα (iota) G2503; κεραία (keraia) G2762; παρέρχομαι (parerchomai) G3928; γίνομαι (ginomai) G1096
scripture.force: Iota = yodh, the smallest Hebrew letter; keraia = the small stroke distinguishing similar letters (kaph from beth, daleth from resh). Jesus contrasts the durability of heaven and earth against these orthographic minutiae using parelthe (subjunctive of parerchomai, 'pass away') and binds the temporal limit to genetai ('come into being / be accomplished'). Scripture's smallest graphic unit is more durable than the cosmos until its purpose is realized

scripture.key_term: κεραία (keraia) G2762; πίπτω (pipto) G4098 inf. πεσεῖν
scripture.force: Lukan parallel to Matt 5:18 with a key lexical difference: where Matthew uses parerchomai of Scripture's potential passing, Luke uses pesein, the infinitive of pipto ('to fall, fail'). Pipto in the canon regularly denotes failure or collapse (Rom 11:11, 1 Cor 10:12, 13:8 'love never fails'). Luke's word choice makes the failure language explicit: not one stroke of the Law can fail

scripture.key_term: γραφή (graphe) G1124; λύω (lyo) G3089 aor. pass. inf. λυθῆναι
scripture.force: Ou dynatai lythenai he graphe = 'Scripture cannot be broken'. Lyo here = to dissolve, annul, set aside; with the modal of impossibility this is a categorical denial that Scripture can be invalidated. Jesus stands an entire argument on a single word in Ps 82:6, demonstrating his confidence that even at the lexical level Scripture binds

scripture.key_term: θεόπνευστος (theopneustos) G2315; γραφή (graphe) G1124; ὠφέλιμος (ophelimos) G5624
scripture.force: Pasa graphe theopneustos kai ophelimos: every Scripture is exhaled by God and is profitable for teaching, reproof, correction, and training in righteousness. The 'cannot mislead in faith and practice' clause of the question stem rests directly on this verse plus its purpose-clause in v. 17, hina artios e ho tou theou anthropos, 'so that the man of God may be complete, equipped for every good work'

scripture.key_term: προφητεία (propheteia) G4394; φέρω (phero) G5342 pres. pass. pt. φερόμενοι; Πνεῦμα Ἅγιον
scripture.force: Ou gar thelemati anthropou enechthe prophteia pote, alla hypo pneumatos hagiou pheromenoi elalesan apo theou anthropoi: no prophecy came by human will, but men were carried along by the Holy Spirit. Pheromenoi (present passive participle of phero) is the same vocabulary used for ships driven by wind (Acts 27:15, 17). The lexical force grounds Scripture's non-failure in the agency of the carrying Spirit, not in human reliability

scripture.key_term: אִמְרָה (imrah) H565; טָהוֹר (tahor) H2889; צָרַף (tsaraph) H6884; זָקַק (zaqaq) H2212
scripture.force: Imrot YHWH amarot tehorot, kesef tsaruph ba-alil la-arets mezuqqaq shivatayim: 'The words of the LORD are pure words, silver refined in a furnace of clay, purified seven times.' Metallurgical metaphor: God's words are tested and proven, free of dross. Pan-canonical concordance link to Prov 30:5 (tsaruph) and Ps 19:7 (tamim)

Lo ish El viykazzeb, u-ben adam ve-yitnecham: 'God is not a man, that he should lie, nor a son of man, that he should change his mind.' Kazab = to lie, deceive. The Balaam oracle states categorically that God does not lie and his word stands. Grounds Scripture's non-misleading character in the speaker's character

Adynaton pseusasthai theon: 'impossible for God to lie.' Stronger modal than Titus 1:2 (apseudes). Pan-canonical concordance link to Num 23:19 and Titus 1:2. If the God who breathed Scripture cannot lie, Scripture cannot mislead in what it teaches

Lema'an tashmor la'asot ke-kol ha-katuv bo, ki az tatslich et-derakeka ve-az taskil: 'so that you may be careful to do according to all that is written in it, for then you will make your way prosperous, and then you will have good success.' Tsalach (same Hifil root as in Isa 55:11) ties the success of the obedient hearer to the success of the word itself. Concordance reinforcement for Isa 55:11's effectual force

'But concerning that day or hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father.' Critics cite this to argue Scripture itself records the Son as not knowing. Best read in context: kenotic-incarnational voluntary limitation of divine prerogative during the earthly ministry (Phil 2:7), not a Scripture-error or a divine misleading. Complicates a naive omniscience-by-attribution argument but not the doctrine that Scripture cannot mislead in faith and practice

Sun 'stood still' over Gibeon. Lexically describes observed sky-phenomena, not a Ptolemaic astronomical claim. Same idiom in Eccl 1:5 (the sun rises and the sun goes down). Complicates a maximalist science-textbook reading but not the proposition that Scripture cannot mislead in faith and practice; the inspired authorial intent is the conquest narrative, not solar mechanics

hermeneutics.notes: Verdict rests on a pan-canonical lemma network: Hebrew davar / asah / tsalach / kazab / tsaraph plus Greek graphe / lyo / parerchomai / pipto / theopneustos / phero / pseudomai. None of the major Protestant or Catholic interpretive frameworks (covenant, dispensational, new-covenant, progressive-covenantalism) produce a divergent verdict on this proposition; they all confess Scripture's infallibility in faith and practice. The Matt 5:18 'until all is accomplished' clause sometimes carries dispensational vs. covenantal weight on the Mosaic Law's ongoing role, but that intramural debate does not touch the infallibility proposition itself, which is what the verse asserts about Scripture's durability against the cosmos. Analogia scripturae is heavily invoked: the Isaiah anchor (effectual word), the Synoptic anchors (graphic-minutiae durability), the Johannine anchor (graphe cannot be broken), and the Pauline/Petrine anchors (theopneustos + phero by the Spirit) together form a self-attesting canonical pattern. Progressive revelation is not a factor because the doctrine concerns Scripture's character qua Scripture, not the unfolding content of revelation.

counter_witness.key_phrase: I have learned to yield this respect and honour only to the canonical books of Scripture: of these alone do I most firmly believe that the authors were completely free from error
the books of Scripture must be acknowledged as teaching solidly, faithfully and without error that truth which God wanted put into sacred writings for the sake of salvation

counter_witness.anchor: Formula of Concord, Epitome, Comprehensive Summary, Rule and Norm 1
counter_witness.key_phrase: the prophetic and apostolic writings of the Old and New Testaments are the only rule and norm according to which all doctrines and all teachers alike must be appraised and judged

counter_witness.anchor: 39 Articles, Article 6 (Of the Sufficiency of the Holy Scriptures for Salvation)
counter_witness.key_phrase: Holy Scripture containeth all things necessary to salvation: so that whatsoever is not read therein, nor may be proved thereby, is not to be required of any man
our full persuasion and assurance of the infallible truth and divine authority thereof is from the inward work of the Holy Spirit bearing witness by and with the Word in our hearts

counter_witness.anchor: Articles of Religion of the Methodist Church, Article 5 (Of the Sufficiency of the Holy Scriptures for Salvation)
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Holy Scriptures contain all things necessary to salvation; so that whatsoever is not read therein, nor may be proved thereby, is not to be required of any man

counter_witness.anchor: Dordrecht Confession 1632, Article 1 (Of God and the Creation of All Things)
counter_witness.key_phrase: we believe with the heart, and confess with the mouth, according to the testimony of holy Scripture, that there is one only, eternal, almighty God

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God, Statement of Fundamental Truths, Article 1 (The Scriptures Inspired)
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Scriptures, both the Old and New Testaments, are verbally inspired of God and are the revelation of God to man, the infallible, authoritative rule of faith and conduct

counter_witness.anchor: Orthodox Church in America, The Orthodox Faith, Volume 1, Doctrine and Scripture, The Bible
counter_witness.key_phrase: All scripture is inspired by God and is profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness (2 Tim 3.16); the Bible is God's authentic self-revelation

web.quote: Isa 55:11 davar H1697 + shuv H7725 negated + asah H6213 + Hifil hitsliah H6743: word does not return empty, accomplishes, prospers in what it was sent for
Matt 5:18 iota G2503 + keraia G2762 + parelthe (parerchomai G3928) + genetai (ginomai G1096): smallest letter and stroke outlast heaven and earth until all is accomplished
Luke 16:17 keraia G2762 + pesein (pipto G4098, to fall, fail): easier for heaven and earth to pass than for one stroke of the Law to fall
John 10:35 ou dynatai lythenai he graphe; lyo G3089 aor. pass. inf. with modal of impossibility: 'Scripture cannot be broken'
2 Tim 3:16 pasa graphe theopneustos G2315 kai ophelimos G5624: every Scripture is God-breathed and profitable for teaching, reproof, correction, training in righteousness
2 Pet 1:21 hypo pneumatos hagiou pheromenoi (phero G5342) elalesan apo theou anthropoi: men spoke from God being carried along by the Holy Spirit
Davar H1697 appears approx. 1441 times in the OT; approx. 394 refer specifically to 'word of the LORD' as authoritative divine speech that is enduring, powerful, and performative
Dei Verbum 11: 'the books of Scripture must be acknowledged as teaching solidly, faithfully and without error that truth which God wanted put into sacred writings for the sake of salvation'
Formula of Concord, Epitome, Rule and Norm: prophetic and apostolic Scriptures are the only rule and standard according to which all dogmas and all teachers must be estimated and judged
OCA, The Orthodox Faith: 'All scripture is inspired by God'; divinely inspired words constitute God's authentic self-revelation to humanity, profitable for teaching, reproof, correction, training
